perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each of the Parties with respect to Confidential Information shall extend for a period of three (3) years following the date of disclosure of that Confidential Information; provided, however, that confidentiality obligations herein shall survive for a period of three (3) years from the date of termination of this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each of the Parties with respect to Confidential Information shall extend for a period of one (1) year following the date of disclosure of that Confidential Information; provided, however, that confidentiality obligations herein shall survive for a period of one (1) year from the date of termination of this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under certain jurisdictions, potentially conflicting with laws designed to safeguard proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. While it doesn't specify a minimum confidentiality period, a one-year confidentiality period could be deemed unreasonably short, especially if the trade secret has a longer economic life, thus undermining the reasonable measures required to protect the trade secret, a condition for recovery under the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, potentially undermining the 'reasonable measures required to protect the trade secret'. The Law Explanation states that a one-year period 'could be deemed unreasonably short, especially if the trade secret has a longer economic life', and therefore weaken the protections provided by the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 18 USC 1836, allows owners of a trade secret to bring a civil action if the trade secret is misappropriated; a shorter, potentially unreasonable confidentiality period could make it more difficult to prove misappropriation and thus enforce the protections offered by the DTSA. This creates an inconsistency between the contract's confidentiality terms and the legal protections intended by trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement on thirty (30) days written notice to the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement on fourteen (14) days written notice to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 14 days could be considered unreasonable and contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in contract law, particularly if significant reliance or investment has been made based on the agreement's continuation.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Terminating an agreement with only 14 days notice, especially if it disrupts ongoing business activities or investments made in reliance on the agreement, could be seen as a violation of the duty of good faith, as it may not provide sufficient time for the other party to mitigate damages or find alternative arrangements. Many jurisdictions interpret this to mean reasonable notice, which may exceed 14 days depending on the context.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 30 to 14 days, which, according to the perturbation explanation and law explanation, could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if significant reliance has been placed on the original agreement. UCC § 1-304, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. A 14-day notice period might not be considered reasonable, thus contradicting this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California introduces potential conflicts if the agreement involves parties or activities outside of California, especially if those activities are legal in England but not in California. This could create a contradiction with the Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution
perturbation.law_citation: U.S. Constitution, Article I, Section 8, Clause 3
perturbation.law_explanation: The Commerce Clause grants Congress the power to regulate commerce among the states and with foreign nations. Applying California law to a contract primarily formed and executed in England, involving only English parties, could violate the Commerce Clause if it unduly burdens foreign commerce or discriminates against it. This is because California law might impose regulations that are inconsistent with international business practices or the laws of England, potentially hindering international trade. While conflict of law rules exist, direct application of California law may overreach if the nexus to California is minimal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Commerce Clause | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Commerce Clause The Commerce Clause refers to Article 1, Section 8, Clause 3 of the U.S. Constitution , which gives Congress the power “to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among states, and with the Indian tribes.” Congress has often used the Commerce Clause to justify exercising legislative power over the activities of states and their citizens, leading to significant and ongoing controversy regarding the balance of power between the federal government and the states. The Commerce Clause has historically been viewed as both a grant of congressional authority and as a restriction on the regulatory authority of the States. The Constitution does not explicitly define the word “commerce” leading to wide debate as to what powers section 8, Clause 3 grants congress. Some argue that it refers simply to trade or exchange, while others claim that the framers of the Constitution intended to describe more broadly commercial and social intercourse between citizens of different states. Courts have generally taken a broad interpretation of the commerce clause for much of United States history. In 1824’s Gibbons v. Ogden , the Supreme Court held that intrastate activity could be regulated under the Commerce Clause, provided that the activity is part of a larger interstate commercial scheme. In 1905’s Swift and Company v. United States , the Supreme Court held that Congress had the authority to regulate local commerce, as long as that activity could become part of a continuous “current” of commerce that involved the interstate movement of goods and services. For a brief period between 1905 and 1937, the Supreme Court narrowed their interpretation of the Commerce Clause in what has now become known as the Lochner era . Courts during this era experimented with the idea that the Commerce Clause does not empower congress to pass laws which impede an individual’s right to enter a business contract. However, beginning with NLRB v. Jones & Laughlin Steel Corp in 1937, the Court began to recognize broader grounds upon which the Commerce Clause could be use
perturbation.justification: The modification, by changing the governing law from England to California, creates a potential contradiction with the Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution, as it could unduly burden foreign commerce. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that applying California law to a contract primarily formed and executed in England, involving only English parties, could violate the Commerce Clause if it unduly burdens foreign commerce or discriminates against it. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines the Commerce Clause as granting Congress the power “to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among states, and with the Indian tribes.” Applying California law may hinder international trade, which is regulated by Congress, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties waive the right to trial by jury in any action, suit, proceeding or counterclaim of any kind arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree to resolve any disputes arising out of or related to this agreement through binding arbitration, waiving the right to trial by jury or any judicial recourse.
perturbation.explanation: While arbitration agreements are generally enforceable, overly broad waivers of legal rights, especially without clear and conspicuous language, can be challenged. This modification eliminates any judicial recourse, which may conflict with laws ensuring fairness and due process in dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA favors arbitration, it doesn't permit agreements that are unconscionable or obtained through fraud or duress. A complete elimination of judicial recourse, without clear and conspicuous language explaining the implications, could be deemed unconscionable, particularly if there is a significant power imbalance between the parties. Courts retain the power to review arbitration agreements for fundamental fairness, and an overly restrictive clause might be struck down as violating public policy by effectively denying a party any meaningful remedy.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces binding arbitration and waives any judicial recourse, creating a potential contradiction with the FAA's allowance for agreements to arbitrate, as highlighted in 9 USC 2. The law explanation suggests that complete elimination of judicial recourse could be deemed unconscionable, impacting the agreement's enforceability. The changed text removes a party's ability to seek judicial review, which could be seen as conflicting with the FAA if it's deemed to deny a meaningful remedy.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Party shall assign this Agreement to a third party without the other Party’s prior written consent, which such consent will not be unreasonably withheld, denied, conditioned or delayed; provided, however, that each Party may assign the rights and obligations to any Group Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Party shall assign this Agreement to a third party under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Prohibiting assignment altogether, even to affiliates, may conflict with statutes or common law principles that protect legitimate business transactions and prevent unreasonable restraints on trade, depending on the jurisdiction and specific circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 317 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts generally allows for the assignment of contract rights, unless the assignment is validly precluded by the contract or statute, or the assignment would materially change the duty of the obligor, or materially increase the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or materially impair his chance of obtaining return performance. A complete prohibition on assignment, even to affiliated entities where the risk and burden on the other party remain essentially the same, could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade and unenforceable, especially if it hinders legitimate business operations. While parties can agree to restrict assignment, courts may scrutinize blanket prohibitions for unreasonableness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: assignment | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you assignment Assignment is a legal term whereby an individual, the “assignor,” transfers rights, property, or other benefits to another, known as the “ assignee .” This concept is used in both contract and property law. The term can refer to either the act of transfer or the rights /property/benefits being transferred. Contract Law Under contract law, assignment of a contract is both an assignment of rights and a delegation of duties in the absence of evidence otherwise. For example, if A contracts with B to teach B guitar for $50, A can assign this contract to C. That is, this assignment is both an assignment of A’s rights under the contract to the $50; and a delegation of A’s duty to teach guitar to C. In this example, A is both the “assignor” and the “delegee” who delegates the duties to another (C), C is known as the “ obligor ” who must perform the obligations to the assignee, and B is the “assignee” who is owed duties and is liable to the “obligor.” Assignment of Rights/Duties Under Contract Law There are a few notable rules regarding assignments under contract law. First, if an individual has not yet secured the contract to perform duties to another, he/she cannot assign his/her future right to an assignee. That is, if A has not yet contracted with B to teach B guitar, A cannot assign his/her rights to C. Second, rights cannot be assigned when they materially change the obligor’s duty and rights. Third, the obligor can sue the assignee directly if the assignee does not pay them. Following the previous example, this means that C (obligor) can sue B (assignee) if C teaches guitar to B, but B does not pay C $50 in return. Delegation of Duties If the promised performance requires a rare genius or skill, then the delegee cannot delegate it to the obligor. It can only be delegated if the promised performance is more commonplace. Further, an obligee can sue if the assignee does not perform. However, the delegee is secondarily liable unless there has been an express release of the delegee. That is, if B does want C to teach guitar but C refuses to, then B can sue C. If C still refuses to perform, then B can compel A to fulfill the duties under secondary liability. Lastly, a related concept is novation , which is when a new obligor substitutes and releases an old obligor. If novation occurs, then the original obligor’s duties are wiped out. However, novation requires an original obligee’s consent . Property Law Under property law, assignment typically arises in landlord-tenant situations. For example, A might be renting from landlord B but wants another party (C) to take over the property. In this scenario, A might be able to choose between assigning and subleasing the property to C. If assigning, A would be giving the entire balance of the term to C, with no reversion to anyone; whereas if subleasing, A would be giving the balance to C for a limited period of the remaining term. Under assignment, C would have privity of estate with the landlord, while under a sublease C would not. [Last reviewed in January of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law trusts inheritances & estates business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text imposes a complete prohibition on assignment, which directly contradicts the principle that contract rights are generally assignable as stated in the Law Explanation, unless validly precluded by the contract or statute, or the assignment would materially change the duty of the obligor. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'Assignment is a legal term whereby an individual, the “assignor,” transfers rights, property, or other benefits to another, known as the “ assignee .”' The original text allowed assignment to group parties, which is now disallowed. This contradicts the general principle of free assignability, making the change a legal contradiction.
